seed_instruction: Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting.
org_constraint: Tailor the email for a professional audience, avoiding jargon and ensuring clarity.
new_constraint: Write the email in a humorous and witty tone.
instruction: Write an email to my boss telling him that I am quitting. Tailor the email for a professional audience, avoiding jargon and ensuring clarity. Write the email in a humorous and witty tone.
response:
thought
Boss.
Resignation (quitting).
Professional audience (needs to be respectful enough not to burn bridges, but the prompt asks for humor/wit).
No jargon, clear.
Humorous and witty.

    *   *Option A: The "Escape Artist" (Playful).* Focuses on the "freedom" aspect.
    *   *Option B: The "Self-Deprecating/Honest" (Lighthearted).* Focuses on the fact that the boss is too good/the job is too much.
    *   *Option C: The "Short & Punchy" (Dry Wit).* Minimalist but funny.

    *   *Subject Line:* Needs to be clear but not alarming. "Resignation" is standard, but maybe something slightly more engaging.
    *   *Opening:* Acknowledge the relationship.
    *   *The News:* State clearly that I'm leaving.
    *   *The Reason (Optional but helpful):* Keep it vague but positive (new adventure).
    *   *The Transition:* Offer to help with the handoff (crucial for professionalism).
    *   *The Closing:* Well wishes.

    *   *Drafting Option 1 (The "New Adventure" approach):*
        "Dear [Boss Name], I have some news. I'm leaving. It's not you, it's me. I'm going to go do something else. I'll help train my replacement so they don't have to suffer as much as I did. Thanks for everything." -> *Too blunt. Needs more wit.*

    *   *Refining Option 1 (The "Breaking Up" vibe):*
        "Subject: It’s not you, it’s me (and a new job offer)."
        "Dear [Boss Name], I’m writing to officially announce my resignation. I’ve decided to pursue a new adventure. I’ve enjoyed my time here, but it’s time for me to see what else is out there. I’ll make sure my desk is clean and my files are organized so you don't have to hunt for them like a treasure map."

    *   *Drafting Option 2 (The "Promotion to Civilian" approach):*
        "Subject: My official retirement from [Company Name]."
        "Dear [Boss Name], I’m writing to let you know that I’m resigning. I’ve decided to trade my [Company Name] badge for a life of [new thing/freedom]. I’ve loved working with you, but my next chapter is calling. I’ll do everything I can to make the transition seamless."

    *   *Drafting Option 3 (The "Short & Witty" approach):*
        "Subject: The Great Escape."
        "Dear [Boss Name], Please accept this as my formal resignation. I’ve decided to see if the grass is actually greener on the other side (or if it’s just better lighting). I’ll be here until [Date] to ensure my successor is fully prepared for the glory of this role."

    *   *Professional?* Yes, they all include a clear resignation date and offer of help.
    *   *No jargon?* Checked.
    *   *Humorous/Witty?* Yes, the "It's not you, it's me" and "Great Escape" tropes work well.

    *   *Self-Correction during selection:* I should provide a few variations so the user can choose the level of "risk" (how funny vs. how safe).

    *   *Variation 1: The "It's Not You, It's Me" (Safe & Sweet).*
    *   *Variation 2: The "New Adventure" (A bit more bold).*
    *   *Variation 3: The "Short & Dry" (For a very chill boss).*Since humor is subjective and depends heavily on your relationship with your boss, I have provided three options ranging from "Safe & Playful" to "Bold & Witty." 

Choose the one that best fits your office culture.

### Option 1: The "It’s Not You, It’s Me" (Safe & Playful)
*Best if you have a friendly, warm relationship with your boss and want to keep things light but very professional.*

**Subject:** It’s not you, it’s me (and a new opportunity)

Dear [Boss's Name],

They say that change is the only constant in life, and I’ve decided to embrace that philosophy by submitting my formal resignation as [Job Title]. My last day will be [Date].

I want to thank you for being such a fantastic mentor. You’ve taught me so much, and while I’m excited to start a new chapter, I’m genuinely sad to be leaving such a great team. I promise to spend my final weeks making sure my transition is so seamless that you’ll barely notice I’m gone (though I’d prefer if you did notice my absence slightly, just to keep my ego intact).

Please let me know how I can best assist in handing over my responsibilities to the next person who is brave enough to take on this role.

Best regards,

[Your Name]

***

### Option 2: The "New Adventure" (Witty & Confident)
*Best if your workplace is high-energy and you want to sound enthusiastic about your next move.*

**Subject:** The Great Escape (or, My Formal Resignation)

Dear [Boss's Name],

I am writing to officially announce that I will be resigning from my position as [Job Title], effective [Date]. 

While I have thoroughly enjoyed my time here—and I’ve truly appreciated your leadership—a new opportunity has come along that I simply can’t turn down. It’s time for me to see if the grass is actually greener on the other side, or if it’s just better lighting over there.

I want to ensure that my departure doesn't leave you in a lurch. I am fully committed to wrapping up my current projects and training my successor to ensure they are prepared for the glory (and the occasional chaos) of this position.

Thank you for everything!

Cheers,

[Your Name]

***

### Option 3: The "Short & Dry" (For a very chill/humorous boss)
*Best if you and your boss frequently joke around and you want to get straight to the point.*

**Subject:** Breaking News: [Your Name] is retiring (from this job)

Dear [Boss's Name],

Please accept this email as formal notification that I am resigning from my position as [Job Title]. My final day of freedom—I mean, employment—will be [Date].

I’ve had a wonderful experience working under your guidance, and I’m grateful for the opportunities I’ve had here. I’d love to make my exit as graceful as possible, so please let me know what I can do over the next [Number] weeks to make the handoff as painless as possible for you.

I’m looking forward to seeing all the great things the team achieves next!

Best,

[Your Name]

***

### A few tips for sending these:
*   **The "In-Person" Rule:** Even with a witty email, it is always best practice to have a quick face-to-face (or video call) conversation before hitting "send." You can say, *"I'm sending over my formal notice now, but I wanted to tell you personally first."*
*   **The Date:** Always clearly state your final day of work to avoid any confusion.
*   **The Transition:** Mentioning your willingness to help with the handoff is the "professional" anchor that keeps the humor from feeling disrespectful.
